config.title: Schöneberg: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on what is actually visible at Schöneberg's famous addresses, when the market runs, and how to plan a half day in the district.

items.q: Is Schöneberg worth visiting as a tourist?
items.a: Yes, with the right expectations. It is best on the second or third day of a trip, once you have seen the historic centre. The strongest reasons to come are Winterfeldtmarkt on a Saturday, the Orte des Erinnerns memorial in the Bayerisches Viertel, and Rathaus Schöneberg with the free Wir waren Nachbarn exhibition. The famous addresses like David Bowie's flat are plaques on private buildings, so treat them as stops along the way, not destinations.

Saturdays from 8:00 to 16:00, which is the full market with around 250 stalls, and Wednesday mornings for a smaller version that winds up in the early afternoon. There is no market on public holidays. Go before 11:00 on Saturday and plan to eat there.

items.q: Can you visit David Bowie's apartment in Berlin?
items.a: No. Hauptstraße 155 is an ordinary lived-in apartment house with a practice on the ground floor. The memorial plaque beside the door, unveiled in 2016, is everything there is to see. Café Neues Ufer next door at number 157, where Bowie actually drank, is the part of the visit where you can sit down. Nearest station is U Kleistpark on the U7.

items.q: What is the memorial in the Bayerisches Viertel?
items.a: Orte des Erinnerns, by Renata Stih and Frieder Schnock, installed in 1993: eighty double-sided signs on lamp posts around Bayerischer Platz. One side shows a simple image, the other the text of a real anti-Jewish law from 1933 to 1945. It is free, always accessible, and orientation boards at Bayerischer Platz and Rathaus Schöneberg map all eighty signs. Nearest station is U Bayerischer Platz on the U4 and U7.

items.q: Can you go up the tower at Rathaus Schöneberg?
items.a: Not on your own. The tower, which holds the Freedom Bell, only opens on guided tours. The bell rings daily at noon for two minutes, which you can hear from the square. Inside the building, the free Wir waren Nachbarn exhibition is open 10:00 to 18:00 every day except Friday.

items.q: Does the Schöneberg Gasometer have a viewing platform?
items.a: Not one you can visit. The roof terrace at about 66 metres is private event space, with no tickets and no opening hours. The 1910 steel frame now wraps an office building on the EUREF business campus. You can walk the campus and look at the frame from below, but do not plan a trip around the view.

items.q: How do I get to Schöneberg from central Berlin?
items.a: Take the U2 from Alexanderplatz or Potsdamer Platz to Nollendorfplatz, about 10 to 12 minutes from Alexanderplatz. Nollendorfplatz is served by four U-Bahn lines, U1, U2, U3 and U4, and the U7 crosses the district at Kleistpark, Eisenacher Straße and Bayerischer Platz. A normal AB ticket covers all of it.
